one_line_takeaway: PRO embeds a jointly-learned (rather than fixed) watermark pattern into open-source LLM weights and explicitly trains against a 'forgotten perturbation' derived from anti-watermarked text, achieving higher detection AUC at lower perplexity than prior learning-based watermarks and materially better retention of watermark AUC after model merging and fine-tuning (e.g., 0.876 vs 0.773 AUC at 0.5 merge ratio; 0.808 vs 0.524 AUC after 1500 fine-tuning steps).
key_finding: On the unaltered LLaMA3-8B model, PRO reaches AUC 0.997 / TPR@5%FPR 0.990 / PPL 4.2, versus the best baseline (Gu et al.-KGW) at AUC 0.991 / TPR@5%FPR 0.956 / PPL 4.9, and the paper reports a 20.5% average perplexity reduction over the best baseline across all three LLMs at matched high AUC (~0.99). Under aggressive model modification the gap widens: at SLERP merge ratio t=0.5 PRO retains AUC 0.876 vs. 0.773 for Gu et al.-KGW and 0.799 for Gloaguen et al.-KGW; after 1500 fine-tuning steps on OpenMathInstruct, PRO retains AUC 0.808 vs. 0.524 for Gu et al.-KTH and 0.721 for Gu et al.-KGW. Quantization and pruning were shown to only mildly degrade all methods (AUC generally stays >=0.98 across methods), so the paper's robustness story rests mainly on the merging/fine-tuning results. Diagnostically, the paper traces watermark forgetting to red-token-dominant gradient updates: fine-tuning on raw or anti-watermarked (red-token-dominant, inverted-logit) text collapses AUC, while fine-tuning on watermark-dominant (green-token) text preserves it -- motivating FPL's anti-watermarked-perturbation training signal. Ablation shows CAWP alone already improves merge-robustness AUC from 0.79 (existing learning-based baseline reference) to 0.82, and adding FPL at alpha=0.1, beta=5 raises merge AUC to 0.87, while larger alpha/beta values (alpha=5, or beta=10) degrade unaltered-AUC and increase perplexity, indicating a robustness/quality tradeoff that requires tuning.
core_contribution: PRO (Precise and Robust Open-source LLM Watermark), a method for embedding text watermarks directly into the weights of open-source LLMs so that standard (unmodified) decoding produces watermarked text, without requiring architectural changes such as enabling output-layer bias terms. It has two components: (1) Co-Adaptive Watermark Policy (CAWP) -- a trainable watermark policy (a BERT embedding model E plus a lightweight MLP mapping model M) that is jointly optimized with the student LLM via KL-divergence distillation from a decoding-based watermark teacher, so the watermark pattern the LLM learns and the pattern used at detection time are the same co-adapted pattern rather than a fixed predefined one (addressing what the paper calls 'Generation-Detection Inconsistency'); and (2) Forgotten Perturbation-aware Learning (FPL) -- a training-time regularizer that identifies 'forgotten perturbation' (weight drift that erases the watermark, empirically traced to gradients from red-token-dominant data) and explicitly trains the model to be robust to a simulated worst-case perturbation derived from anti-watermarked (red-token-dominant) text, solved with three forward/backward passes rather than full second-order optimization.
threat_model: An LLM owner publicly releases an open-source model and wants to later verify whether a piece of generated text originated from it. Once released, users have full white-box access to weights and architecture and may apply fine-tuning, model merging, quantization, or pruning (intentionally or incidentally) to remove or degrade the watermark. The owner retains a private, prompt-independent detection mechanism (the co-optimized mapping model M), possibly exposed as a detection API, that scores generated text alone -- no access to the user's input prompt and no control over the decoding process at inference time. Decoding-based watermarking (bias added during sampling) is explicitly noted as inapplicable here because open-source users control decoding and can strip it.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: (1) the CAWP convergence proof (Appendix D) rests on assumptions -- Lipschitz smoothness of the LLM and MLP gradients, bounded policy outputs/gradients, and convexity of the L1-based normalization regularizer -- that are asserted rather than empirically verified; (2) FPL's second-order-derivative optimization is approximated with a single normalized gradient step (three forward/backward passes) rather than solved exactly, an approximation whose error is not separately quantified; (3) the ablation shows robustness and quality trade off against each other and are sensitive to alpha/beta, i.e., there is no single hyperparameter setting that is simultaneously best on all axes; (4) detection still assumes access to a private, co-optimized mapping model M and its embedding model E -- if an adversary can query or approximate M/E (e.g., via the detection API), the security of the scheme against adaptive removal attacks is not analyzed in the main text; (5) robustness is only evaluated against merging with the original unwatermarked base model, quantization, pruning, and supervised fine-tuning on two datasets -- no adaptive/adversarial attacker who specifically targets CAWP's learned mapping model is evaluated in the main body (paraphrasing robustness is deferred to the Appendix, not examined in the pages reviewed here); (6) the paper states it evaluates 3 LLMs up to 8B parameters, leaving open whether results hold at larger production scales.

datasets_used: C4 (Dodge et al., 2021) — 50-token prefixes used to generate 5,000 watermarked and 5,000 non-watermarked 200-token continuations for detectability/quality evaluation
OpenMathInstruct — used as fine-tuning data to test watermark robustness under downstream fine-tuning
OpenCodeInstruct — used as a second fine-tuning dataset to test robustness
quantitative_results: Unaltered LLaMA3-8B: PRO AUC 0.997, TPR@5%FPR 0.990, PPL 4.2 vs. best baseline Gu et al.-KGW AUC 0.991, TPR@5%FPR 0.956, PPL 4.9
PRO achieves 20.5% lower perplexity than the best baseline while matching ~0.99 AUC, averaged across the three tested LLMs
SLERP merge at t=0.5: PRO AUC 0.876 vs. Gu et al.-KGW 0.773, Gloaguen et al.-KGW 0.799, Gu et al.-KTH 0.563
SLERP merge at t=0.9: PRO AUC 0.68 vs. Gu et al.-KGW 0.558, Gloaguen et al.-KGW 0.588, Gu et al.-KTH 0.513
Fine-tuning on OpenMathInstruct, 1500 steps: PRO AUC 0.808 vs. Gu et al.-KTH 0.524, Gu et al.-KGW 0.721, Gloaguen et al.-KGW 0.718
Fine-tuning on OpenCodeInstruct, 1500 steps: PRO AUC 0.821 vs. Gu et al.-KTH 0.533, Gu et al.-KGW 0.754, Gloaguen et al.-KGW 0.782
Quantization (4-bit HQQ) on unaltered watermark: PRO AUC 0.995 vs. Gu et al.-KGW 0.992, Gloaguen et al.-KGW 0.983, Gu et al.-KTH 0.882
Pruning (Wanda, ρ=0.5): PRO AUC 0.990 vs. Gu et al.-KGW 0.973, Gloaguen et al.-KGW 0.987, Gu et al.-KTH 0.921
CAWP watermark mapping model M is a lightweight MLP with 1.16M parameters (0.0388% of a 3B LLM)
PRO reaches AUC 0.997 within 2000 training steps vs. KGW-based baseline needing 5000 steps for maximum AUC of 0.991
Ablation (merge AUC, t=0.5): alpha=0 (CAWP only) gives merge AUC 0.82; alpha=0.1, beta=5 (full FPL) gives merge AUC 0.87; alpha=5 degrades merge AUC to 0.79 and unaltered PPL to 5.00
Ablation on beta: beta=0 gives merge AUC 0.82; beta=5 gives merge AUC 0.87 (best); beta=10 gives merge AUC 0.84 with PPL rising to 4.52
baselines_compared: Gu et al. (2023) distilled with KGW watermark, three (n,delta) configurations: (1,2), (0,1), (1,1)
Gu et al. (2023) distilled with KTH (Kuditipudi et al. 2023 exponential distortion-free scheme)
Gloaguen et al. (2025) — KGW embedded into 'stable' parameters identified via contrastive task-vector analysis before/after fine-tuning
